title: The Decameron
description: A fourteenth-century frame narrative of storytelling, desire, wit, and social satire.

cover.alt: The Decameron cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A fourteenth-century frame narrative of storytelling, desire, wit, and social satire.
banningParagraphs: The Decameron entered censorship debates as a story collection associated with sexuality, anti clerical satire, and storytelling. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around obscenity and morality.

The earliest event currently captured here is 20th century in Australia, where Australian customs and censors banned importation. Its erotic and anti-clerical material brought the classic into modern obscenity regimes. The case shows how medieval literature could still be treated as a live threat by modern censors.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: Literature Suppressed on Sexual Grounds
counterReadings.author: Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: Surveys the legal and moral language used to suppress books as obscene.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for seeing how obscenity law and censorship habits changed over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
